text.title: AI Code Lint Presets Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every adoption of these presets MUST enforce. Hard rules; agents and humans MUST NOT silently downgrade them.

rule.statement: The two preset files (`ruff-ai-presets.toml`, `eslint-ai-presets.json`) are versioned in the repo and changed only via PR review. The AI assistant is FORBIDDEN from editing these files; a check (CODEOWNERS rule and a CI guard) MUST refuse PRs that modify them without human approval.
rule.rationale: If the AI can relax its own lint, it will. Multiple repositories have observed AI assistants adding `# noqa` or downgrading ESLint rule severity to silence findings in their own PR. CODEOWNERS protection is the cheapest defence.

rule.statement: Tests that contain only `assert True`, `assert response`, `expect(result)`, or other no-op assertions MUST fail lint. Ruff rule: custom `T-NO-OP-ASSERT`. ESLint: custom rule via `eslint-plugin-jest/expect-expect` or equivalent. Tests that pass without checking specific properties are a tax, not coverage.
rule.rationale: "Generate tests for this module" is the most common LLM request and the most common smell source. Tests with property assertions catch regressions; no-op tests train developers to ignore the suite.

rule.statement: `try: ... except: pass`, `except Exception: pass`, or `catch(e) {}` MUST fail lint. Ruff: BLE001 + E722. ESLint: `no-empty` + `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any` on the catch parameter. An empty broad except is FORBIDDEN; the developer MUST handle, log, or re-raise with an explicit narrower type.

rationale.source: Python idioms PEP-8; ECMAScript catch-binding semantics

rule.rationale: Empty broad excepts hide hallucinated APIs (the AI's `requests.get()` call fails — the except silently swallows). The PR looks green; production fails opaque. Broad-except detection is the highest-leverage lint rule against AI-introduced silent failure.

rule.statement: Magic numbers / strings used as policy values (timeouts, retry counts, thresholds, URLs, secret-prefixes) MUST be replaced with named constants. Ruff: PLR2004. ESLint: `no-magic-numbers`. Test files and obviously-data values (HTTP status codes 200/404/500 in assertions) are exempted.
rule.rationale: LLMs emit literals where humans use constants. `time.sleep(2)` reappears in 18 files of an AI-drafted codebase; the constant `RETRY_BACKOFF_SECONDS = 2` reappears never. Magic-literal lint forces the AI to refactor.

rule.statement: `print()`, `console.log()`, `debugger;`, `breakpoint()`, and `pp ` (Ruby) MUST fail lint in non-test code. Ruff: T20. ESLint: `no-console`, `no-debugger`. Production code uses the project's logger; tests may use print for diagnostic output, scripts (`/scripts/*`) may be exempted.
rule.rationale: AI assistants insert print / console.log during inner-loop debugging and forget to remove. The smell is detectable; the lint rule is the cheapest scrubber.

example.title: Broad except caught and repaired
example.note: r3 satisfied. Narrow exception class, logging with structure, re-raise. The repair also surfaces the magic-literal rule (timeout becomes a constant) per r4.

example.title: No-op test that lint MUST catch
example.note: r2 violation. `assert user` is true for any non-null object. Repair: assert specific properties (user.id is not None, user.name == "alice", user.created_at < now()).

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the preconditions in AGENTS.md "Applies If" cannot be met, or when a more specific methodology covers the exact use case better.
rule.rationale: A methodology applied outside its preconditions creates noise; the decision tree routes obvious non-matches to skip rather than forcing a fit.
